result.markdown:
--- title: "Trend analysis with `eawsAnalysis`" --- ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # install.packages("remotes") # remotes::install_github("will02-code/eawsAnalysis") library(eawsAnalysis) library(magrittr) library(readr) ``` ::: #### Example analysis We'll walk through an example analysis using the `eawsAnalysis` package. The same process was repeated for all metrics and scales. There are four main steps to the analysis: 1. Define parameters 2. Clean data 3. Run models 4. Clean output We have provided code snippets for each step below, but generally we recommend using the `clean_data_run_models()` function which combines all steps into one function call. ##### Define parameters Here we define the parameters for the analysis. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-summary="Define analysis parameters"} programs = c("Eastern Australian Survey") # <1> dataset = data_clean # <2> metric = "abundance" # <3> wetlands = NULL # <4> valleys = NULL # <5> basinDiv = NULL # <5> grouping_cols = "spp_code" # <6> decade_analysis = FALSE # <7> by_condition = NULL # <8> ``` ::: 1. Survey programs to include. 2. Dataset to use. 3. Metric to analyse. 4. Wetlands to include (`NULL` for all). 5. Valleys and basin divisions to include (`NULL` for all). 6. Grouping column (`NULL` for overall analysis). 7. Whether to run decade-wise analysis (`TRUE`/`FALSE`). 8. Surface water condition column to group by (e.g. `"basin_condition"`, `"wetland_condition"`); `NULL` for no grouping. ##### Clean data We need the data to be cleaned before running models. This involves filtering to the specified programs, wetlands, valleys, and grouping column, as well as calculating the metric of interest. See `get_bird_data()` function for details. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-summary="Clean data for analysis"} d2 <- get_bird_data( dataset, programs = programs, metric = metric, wetlands = wetlands, valleys = valleys, basinDiv = basinDiv, grouping_cols = grouping_cols, by_condition = by_condition ) ``` ::: ##### Run models We then run the specified models on the cleaned data. See `run_mk_models()` function for details. We use the grouping column, and decade analysis parameters. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-summary="Run statistical models"} output <- run_mk_models( d2, decade_analysis = decade_analysis, grouping_cols = grouping_cols, ) ``` ::: ##### Clean output Finally, we clean the output from the models to extract relevant results, trends, and predictions. See `clean_model_output()` function for details. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-summary="Clean model output"} cleaned <- clean_model_output( output, decade_analysis = decade_analysis, grouping_cols = grouping_cols ) ``` ::: ##### Make plots and summary stats We can then make plots of the fitted models and calculate summary statistics using the `post_fitting_line_plot()` function and summarisation code within `clean_data_run_models()`. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-fold="true" code-summary="Generate plots and summary statistics"} group_vars <- c( if (decade_analysis) "decade", if (!is.null(grouping_cols)) grouping_cols, if (!is.null(by_condition)) by_condition ) cleaned_summary <- cleaned %>% dplyr::ungroup() %>% dplyr::summarise( min = min(value), q1 = stats::quantile(value, 0.25), median = stats::median(value), q3 = stats::quantile(value, 0.75), max = max(value), long_term_avg = mean(value), dplyr::across( tidyselect::any_of(c( "percent_estimate", "percent_p.value", "mk_tau", "mk_p_value", 'mk_logslope', 'mk_percentslope', 'mk_intercept', "n_rows" )), dplyr::first ), .by = tidyselect::all_of(group_vars) ) p <- post_fitting_line_plot( cleaned, decade_analysis = decade_analysis, grouping_cols = grouping_cols, metric = metric ) p ``` ::: ###### Complete analysis function To simplify the analysis process, there is a wrapper function `clean_data_run_models()` which combines all steps into one function call. This function takes in the dataset and analysis parameters, cleans the data, runs the models, cleans the output, generates plots, and calculates summary statistics. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-summary="Run complete analysis in one function"} result <- clean_data_run_models( dataset = data_clean, programs = c("Eastern Australian Survey"), metric = "abundance", wetlands = NULL, valleys = NULL, grouping_cols = NULL, # Grouping column (e.g. "spp_code" for species-level analysis) basinDiv = NULL, decade_analysis = FALSE, trend_line = TRUE # Whether to generate trend line plot ) cleaned_data <- result[[1]] plot <- result[[2]] summary_stats <- result[[3]] ``` ::: So we end up with three outputs: - `cleaned_data`: Cleaned data frame with model results and predictions - `plot`: ggplot2 object of fitted model plot - `summary_stats`: Data frame containing compiled trend statistics (e.g., Mann-Kendall tau, p-values). **Full data frame** ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: **Fitted model plot** ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](trend_analysis_files/figure-html/fitted_model_plot-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: **Summary statistics** ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ##### Or with functional groups ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code code-summary="Run complete analysis in one function"} result_fx <- clean_data_run_models( dataset = data_clean, programs = c("Eastern Australian Survey"), metric = "abundance", wetlands = NULL, valleys = NULL, grouping_cols = "fx_group", basinDiv = NULL, decade_analysis = FALSE, trend_line = TRUE ) cleaned_data_fx <- result_fx[[1]] plot_fx <- result_fx[[2]] summary_stats_fx <- result_fx[[3]] ``` ::: **Full data frame** ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: **Fitted model plot** ::: {.cell .fig-cap-location-top} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Fitted Model Plot](trend_analysis_files/figure-html/fitted-model-plot-fx-group-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: **Summary statistics** ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ##### We can also get trends by SWC To do this, we need to calculate SWC first and join it to the dataset ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` Rows: 874 Columns: 5 ── Column specification ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────── Delimiter: "," chr (1): valley_name dbl (4): year, inundated_area, valley_surface_water, valley_surface_water_la... ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data. ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message. Rows: 1406 Columns: 5 ── Column specification ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────── Delimiter: "," chr (1): wetland_name dbl (4): year, inundated_area, wetland_surface_water, wetland_surface_water_... ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data. ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message. Rows: 38 Columns: 4 ── Column specification ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────── Delimiter: "," dbl (4): year, inundated_area, basin_surface_water, basin_surface_water_lagged ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data. ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message. ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
